questions.question: How does a pipe organ produce sound?
options: Air forced through pipes by a wind system

questions.question: The largest pipe in a typical pipe organ can be how long?
What is the name for the keyboard played with the feet on a pipe organ?
What percentage of U.S. electricity currently comes from renewable sources?
What is the main challenge of integrating solar and wind into the power grid?
options: Their output is intermittent and hard to predict
They can only operate in warm climates

questions.question: What does "peak demand" refer to in electrical grid management?
options: The maximum voltage the grid can carry
The highest power consumption period of the day
The efficiency rating of a power plant
The distance electricity can travel without loss

talks.title: Fungi: The Internet of the Forest
talks.speaker: Dr. Maya Chen

questions.question: What is the "Wood Wide Web"?
options: Underground fungal networks connecting tree root systems
A type of bark beetle communication system

questions.question: Mycelium is the term for which part of a fungus?
options: The network of thread-like root structures

questions.question: What do trees share through fungal networks?

originStory.text:
Hi. I'm Justin Fritz — and I'm a Nerd Nite Boss.

That's a real title. The Nerd Nite organization gave it to me, and I take it very seriously.

Nerd Nite started in Boston in 2003. A guy named Matt Wasowski talked a bar into letting scientists give talks between band sets. It spread to over 100 cities. And it came to Fort Collins because of me and one other very important person.

His name is Senne Van Loon.

But first: trivia.

Senne and I started Nerd Nite Fort Collins together.

Senne is the kind of person who hears "let's put on a nerdy event at a bar" and immediately says yes — no hesitation, full commitment. That energy is rare and it matters. You can't build something like this alone.

Our early events were scrappy. We found speakers by pestering our networks, borrowed equipment, and hoped the venue's Wi-Fi held up for the slide decks. Sometimes it didn't.

It didn't matter. The talks were great. That's always been the thing.

We didn't stay a two-person operation for long.

Jamie Fritz and Hannah Bauer joined us — and the whole thing leveled up. More hands, better events, a real team.

For a while, Mary Beth Arcodia hosted. She was brilliant at it — warm, sharp, funny in exactly the right way for a room full of nerds on their third drink. She held the stage like she was born for it.

Mary Beth eventually left for a faculty position in Miami. We were sad to lose her and proud of her in equal measure. That's the Nerd Nite family.

You just played Nerdometer.

Every edition is built around a real Nerd Nite Fort Collins event — the actual talks, the actual speakers, the actual obsessions people brought to a bar and shared with strangers.

Senne, Jamie, Hannah, Mary Beth — and everyone who has ever stood up at a Nerd Nite and trusted a room full of people with the thing they love most.

That's what this is. Come be part of it.

— Justin Fritz, Nerd Nite Fort Collins Boss
